subtopic: surface area of sphere vs cylinders
question: The surface area of a solid sphere of radius R is equal to the total surface area of 10 solid closed cylinders of radius r and height 4r. Which of the following is an expression for R in terms of r? (The surface area of a sphere of radius $R$ is $4\pi R^2$.)

steps.title: Each cylinder s total surface area
steps.content: Each cylinder's total surface area = $2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r(4r) = 10\pi r^2$.

steps.title: Set equal to sphere
steps.content: Set equal to sphere: $4\pi R^2 = 100\pi r^2 \Rightarrow R^2 = 25r^2 \Rightarrow R = 5r$.

question: Which of the following is a correct rearrangement of $y = p - \dfrac{q-r}{s-x}$ to make x the subject?

A spaceship of mass 10000 kg is moving at 2.0 m/s relative to a space station. The spaceship is captured by a robotic arm attached to the space station and brought to rest by a force of 1000 N. How far will the spaceship move in its initial direction relative to the space station while the force is being applied? (Assume that the acceleration of the space station is negligible.)

A circuit has a battery, an ammeter, and three identical resistors. [DIAGRAM: battery in series with one resistor, then two further identical resistors arranged so one branch contains a switch; ammeter reads total circuit current]. When the switch is open, the reading on the ammeter is 1.0 A and the power transferred from the battery is 1.0 W. The switch is now closed. What is the new reading on the ammeter and what is the new power transferred from the battery?

steps.title: With switch open current flows through resistors in
steps.content: With switch open, current flows through 2 resistors in series (3rd branch open): total resistance $2R$, $one=1.0$A so EMF $= 2R$, and $P=one^2(2R)=1.0$W gives $2R=1\,\Omega$ so $R=0.5\,\Omega$, EMF $=1.0$V.

steps.title: Closing the switch puts the third identical resistor
steps.content: Closing the switch puts the third identical resistor in parallel with one of the existing resistors, giving total resistance $R + R/2 = 1.5R = 0.75\,\Omega$.

steps.title: Total current A power W W
The ammeter in the branch shared by both
steps.content: The ammeter (in the branch shared by both resistors, not the parallel pair) reads half of this branch current: $1.33/2\approx0.67$A.

subtopic: area of a triangle inside a square using ratios
question: WXYZ is a square of side length 1. Points M, N, P lie on sides WX, XY, YZ respectively (in that cyclic order around the square), with WM:MX = 1:2, XN:NY = 3:1, YP:PZ = 4:1. [DIAGRAM: square WXYZ with points M, N, P marked on three sides as described]. What is the area of triangle MNP?

subtopic: elastic potential energy graph, spring constant
question: A spring is initially unstretched. A force F is used to stretch the spring. The extension x and the energy E stored in the stretched spring are measured for different values of F. The graph shows how the energy E, in J, varies with the extension squared, $x^2$, in cm$^2$: a straight line through the origin passing through the point $(25, 0.015)$. What is the magnitude of F when the spring stores 0.015 J of energy?

steps.title: For a spring so the slope of E
steps.content: For a spring, $E = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$, so the slope of E vs $x^2$ is $k/2$.

steps.title: At this point m so N
steps.content: At this point $x = 0.05$ m, so $F = kx = 12 \times 0.05 = 0.60$ N.

question: Given that $\dfrac{27^{2(x-2)}}{9^{(2x-3)}} = (81)^{3/2}$, what is the value of $x$?

steps.title: Write everything as powers of and

question: A solid, cylindrical metal bar has a uniform cross-sectional area of 12 cm$^2$ and a volume of 180 cm$^3$. The bar rests on a horizontal surface on one of its circular faces. The pressure on the surface due to the bar is 0.45 N/cm$^2$. What is the density of the metal, in g/cm$^3$? (gravitational field strength = 10 N/kg)

steps.title: Weight N so mass kg g
steps.content: Weight $= P \times A = 0.45 \times 12 = 5.4$ N, so mass $= 5.4/10 = 0.54$ kg $= 540$ g.

question: Last year, the salary of the coach of a football club was 80% of the salary of the star player. At the start of the new year, the coach received a 15% increase in salary and the star player received a 38% increase in salary. What percentage of the star player's new salary is the coach's new salary?

Two samples of pure radioactive isotopes X and Y decay with half-lives of 2 days and 3 days, respectively. Both X and Y decay in a single step into different stable isotopes. Initially the number of atoms of X is twice the number of atoms of Y. After how many days are the expected numbers of atoms of X and Y equal to each other?

options.A: The expected numbers of atoms of X and Y are never equal.

question: An athlete's training session consists of several complete repetitions of a three-part programme: 1. Walk 100 m at an average speed of 6 km/h. 2. Jog 200 m at an average speed of 10 km/h. 3. Run 100 m at an average speed of 20 km/h. What is the athlete's average speed for the complete training session, in km/h?

steps.title: Using a common denominator of h
steps.content: Using a common denominator of 600: $10/600+12/600+3/600 = 25/600 = 1/24$ h.

subtopic: steady-state temperature gradient through two materials
question: A large, flat, metal plate is coated on one side with a layer of thermally insulating material of the same thickness a as the metal plate. The uninsulated top surface of the metal plate is maintained at a constant temperature $T_1$. The bottom surface of the insulating material is maintained at a constant, lower temperature $T_2$. The system is in equilibrium. [DIAGRAM: metal plate (thickness a) on top of insulating layer (thickness a); temperature varies from $T_1$ at top to $T_2$ at bottom]. Which graph could show how the temperature varies with distance from the top surface of the metal plate to the bottom surface of the insulating material?

options.A: [DIAGRAM: graph showing a very shallow/near-flat temperature drop across the metal (0 to a, good conductor) then a steep near-linear drop across the insulator (a to 2a)]
options.B: [DIAGRAM: graph showing a steep drop across the metal, then shallow drop across the insulator]
options.C: [DIAGRAM: graph showing linear uniform drop across the entire distance 0 to 2a]
options.D: [DIAGRAM: graph showing no change across metal, sudden step change at a, then flat]
options.E: [DIAGRAM: graph showing curved (non-linear) drop across both sections]

steps.title: In steady state the same rate of heat
steps.content: In steady state, the same rate of heat flow passes through both layers (same thickness a, same area).

steps.title: Metal is a good conductor low thermal resistance
steps.content: Metal is a good conductor (low thermal resistance) so very little temperature drop occurs across it; the insulator has high thermal resistance so almost all the temperature drop $(T_1-T_2)$ occurs across it, roughly linearly since it's a uniform material at steady state.

steps.title: This matches graph A near flat over the
steps.content: This matches graph A: near-flat over the metal (0 to a), then a steep near-linear decrease over the insulator (a to 2a).

subtopic: similar solids, surface area and volume ratios
question: Two objects X and Y are similar. The surface area of object Y is double the surface area of object X. The volume of object Y is $7\sqrt{2}$ cm$^3$ more than the volume of object X. What is the volume of object X, in cm$^3$?

steps.title: Linear scale factor satisfies so and volume ratio
steps.content: Linear scale factor $k$ satisfies $k^2=2$ so $k=\sqrt{2}$, and volume ratio $k^3=2\sqrt{2}$.

steps.title: Let be X s volume V X V
steps.content: Let $V_X$ be X's volume: $2\sqrt{2}\,V_X - V_X = 7\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow V_X(2\sqrt{2}-1)=7\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow V_X = \dfrac{7\sqrt{2}}{2\sqrt{2}-1}

question: The voltage output of a power station is stepped up using a transformer before the power is transmitted to a distant town. The primary coil of this transformer has 300 turns and the secondary coil has 1500 turns. In the town, a step-down transformer reduces the voltage supplied by the transmission cables to 33 000 V for distribution within the town. The step-down transformer supplies a current of 1500 A. The current in the transmission cables is 450 A and both transformers are ideal and 100% efficient. What is the voltage output of the power station? (Assume that the resistance of the transmission cables is negligible.)

steps.title: This equals the power on the transmission line
steps.content: This equals the power on the transmission line, so line voltage $= P/450 = 110000$ V.

steps.title: This is the secondary voltage of the step
steps.content: This is the secondary voltage of the step-up transformer, which steps up by a factor $1500/300=5$.

question:
The equation
$\left(\dfrac{a\times10^4+2a\times10^3}{3\times10^{-1}}\right)^2=8\times10^9$
has two solutions for $a$. What is the positive difference between these two solutions?

steps.title: The positive difference between the two solutions is
steps.content: The positive difference between the two solutions is $2\sqrt{5}$.

subtopic: transverse wave, average particle speed over integer cycles
question: A transverse wave with an amplitude of 3.0 cm travels along a stretched string. The wave has a frequency of 12 Hz and a wavelength of 0.25 m. What is the average speed of a particle in the string as the string oscillates during a time of 2.0 s?

steps.title: In s there are exactly complete cycles
steps.content: In 2.0 s there are exactly $2.0/T = 24$ complete cycles.

steps.title: Each cycle a particle travels a total path
steps.content: Each cycle a particle travels a total path of $4A = 4\times3.0=12$ cm (up to crest, back through zero to trough, back to zero).

steps.title: Total distance cm over s giving average speed
steps.content: Total distance $=24\times12=288$ cm over 2.0 s, giving average speed $=288/2.0=144$ cm/s.

subtopic: vectors, ratio division of a line segment
question: X and Y are the end-points of a line segment. Point P has coordinates $(-8, 5)$. P lies on the line segment XY such that XP:PY is 1:2 and $\overrightarrow{XP} = \begin{pmatrix}4\\-3\end{pmatrix}$. A point Q is such that $\overrightarrow{QY} = \begin{pmatrix}7\\6\end{pmatrix}$. What are the coordinates of point Q?

A battery and two resistors X and Y are connected in series. The power transferred by the battery is 6 W. The resistance of X is 10 $\Omega$. The voltage across Y is 4 V. What is the current in the circuit?

steps.title: Factoring so A taking the positive root
steps.content: Factoring: $(5I-3)(one+1)=0$, so $one = 3/5$ A (taking the positive root).

question: Find the maximum value of $2^{\sin x} \times 3^{-\sin x}$ where $0° \le x \le 360°$.

steps.title: Since this is maximised when is as small
steps.content: Since $\dfrac23<1$, this is maximised when $\sin x$ is as small as possible, i.e. $\sin x=-1$ (at $x=270^\circ$).

question: A diver at the bottom of a lake of depth d fills a syringe with an ideal gas and seals the nozzle. The piston remains free to move. The volume of the gas in the syringe at the bottom of the lake is 90 cm$^3$. As the diver returns to the surface, the temperature of the gas does not change. At the surface of the lake the gas in the syringe is at atmospheric pressure and the volume of the gas is 720 cm$^3$. What is the volume of the gas in the syringe at a depth $d/2$?

steps.title: Since pressure increases linearly with depth
steps.content: Since pressure increases linearly with depth, $P_{bottom}=P_{atm}+\rho g d = 8P_{atm} \Rightarrow \rho g d = 7P_{atm}$.

question: $x^2-x-6$ is a factor of $x^3+ax^2+2x+b$, where a and b are real constants. What is the value of $a+b$?

Two pipes contain air at the same temperature and pressure. A stationary sound wave is formed in the first pipe, which is closed at one end and open at the other end. The lowest frequency of stationary sound wave that can be formed in this pipe is 4000 Hz. The second pipe has the same length as the first pipe, but is open at both ends. What is the lowest frequency of stationary sound wave that can be formed in the second pipe?

steps.title: Closed pipe fundamental Hz so
steps.content: Closed pipe fundamental: $f_1 = v/4L = 4000$ Hz, so $L=v/16000$.

steps.title: Open pipe fundamental Hz
steps.content: Open pipe fundamental: $f = v/2L = v/(2\times v/16000) = 8000$ Hz.

question: An arithmetic progression has first term a and common difference d. The sum of the first 9 terms plus the sum of the first 10 terms is equal to the sum of the first 11 terms. Which of the following is a correct expression for a in terms of d?

A cannon ball of mass 2.6 kg is fired horizontally from a cannon on the top of a cliff at a speed of 90 m/s. The height of the cliff above the horizontal ground below is 45 m. What is the magnitude of the impulse that acts on the ball between leaving the cannon and reaching the ground? (gravitational field strength = 10 N/kg; assume that air resistance is negligible)

steps.title: Vertical velocity gained m s horizontal velocity is
steps.content: Vertical velocity gained: $v_y=gt=30$ m/s (horizontal velocity is unchanged, so impulse is purely due to the vertical velocity change).

question: The first three terms of a convergent geometric progression are: $2p$, $p-3$, $p-7$. What is the sum to infinity of this progression?

steps.title: For terms are ratio convergent first term sum
steps.content: For $p=9$: terms are $18, 6, 2$, ratio $r=1/3$ (convergent), first term $a=18$, sum to infinity $=18/(1-1/3)=27$.

steps.title: For terms are ratio divergent rejected

subtopic: friction on an incline, power dissipated
question: A child on a sledge is travelling straight down a snow-covered slope at a constant speed of 15 m/s. The mass of the child and sledge together is 60 kg. The angle of the slope to the horizontal is 30°. What is the rate at which thermal energy is being produced due to friction forces? (gravitational field strength = 10 N/kg)

steps.title: Constant speed means friction balances the component of
steps.content: Constant speed means friction balances the component of gravity along the slope: $f = mg\sin\theta = 60\times10\times\sin30^\circ

steps.title: Rate of thermal energy production W
steps.content: Rate of thermal energy production $= f\times v = 300\times15=4500$ W.

question: The diagram shows a circle with centre O, with an angle of 40° and an angle of 55° marked, and x is the angle to be found. [DIAGRAM: circle with centre O; two chords/radii forming angles 40° and 55° with an inscribed or central angle x]. What is the value of x?

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Using circle theorems angles subtended by the same
steps.content: Using circle theorems (angles subtended by the same arc, and the fact that the angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference, together with the given 40^\circ and 55^\circ), the value of x that satisfies the geometric constraints in the diagram is 75^\circ.

question: The diagram shows a battery with no internal resistance and four identical resistors P, Q, R and S. [DIAGRAM: resistor network with P, Q, R, S arranged in a combination of series/parallel branches connected to the battery]. Resistor Q dissipates 4.0 W of power. What is the total power supplied by the battery?

steps.title: Read the network topology
steps.content: The four identical resistors of resistance $R$ form the chain $P$ in series with the parallel pair $Q\parallel R$, then $S$ in series. The equivalent resistance is $$R_{\mathrm{eq}} = R + \dfrac{R}{2} + R = \dfrac{5}{2}R.$$

steps.title: Relate battery current to power in Q
steps.content: Battery current $I$ splits equally through $Q$ and $R$, so each parallel branch carries $\dfrac{I}{2}$. Power in $Q$ is $$\left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right)^2 R = \dfrac{I^2 R}{4} = 4.0\,\mathrm{W} \implies I^2 R = 16\,\mathrm{W}.$$

question: Find the real value of $x$ that satisfies $\log_3(x^2+3x+2)=2+\log_3(x^2+2x)$.

steps.title: Combine logs log x x x x so
This gives x x discriminant x so x
Checking domain both log arguments must be positive
steps.content: Checking domain (both log arguments must be positive): x=-2 gives x^2+2x=0, undefined; x=1/8 gives both arguments positive.

question: Two identical, uniform, thin planks, each of mass 40 kg, are propped up against one another in equilibrium on a rough surface, forming an A-frame shape. Each plank is at an angle of 30° to the vertical. [DIAGRAM: two planks leaning against each other at their tops, each at 30° to vertical, with point P marked at the base of one plank on the ground]. What is the magnitude of the friction force at point P? (gravitational field strength = 10 N/kg)

steps.title: Set up forces on one plank
steps.content: Each plank has weight $W = 40 \times 10 = 400\,\mathrm{N}$ and makes $30^\circ$ with the vertical. By symmetry the apex reaction is horizontal, so the normal reaction at the base equals $W$. Friction $f$ at the base balances that horizontal apex force.

steps.title: Take moments about the apex
steps.content: With plank length $L$, moments about the apex give $$W \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}\sin 30^\circ = f \cdot L\cos 30^\circ.$$

subtopic: definite integral involving a square root
question: Which of the following is equal to $\displaystyle\int_{-(3-\sqrt5)}^{3-\sqrt5} \frac{x^2}{3-\sqrt5}\,dx$?

subtopic: Newton's third law, blocks in contact
question: Three blocks P, Q and R are in contact on a horizontal surface, in the order P-Q-R, with a horizontal force applied to P. The mass of P is 4.0 kg and the masses of Q and R are each 2.0 kg. A horizontal force of 20 N is applied to P so that all three blocks move together. The friction force between P and the surface is 2.0 N, and the friction forces between Q and R and the surface are each 1.0 N. What is the magnitude of the force that R exerts on Q?

steps.title: Total mass kg total friction N
steps.content: Total mass $=8.0$ kg, total friction $=2.0+1.0+1.0=4.0$ N.

steps.title: Consider block R alone the only horizontal forces
steps.content: Consider block R alone: the only horizontal forces are the push from Q, $N_{QR}$, and friction $1.0$ N opposing motion.

steps.title: Newton s second law N
steps.content: Newton's second law: $N_{QR}-1.0 = m_R a = 2.0\times2.0=4.0 \Rightarrow N_{QR}=5.0$ N.

steps.title: By Newton s third law the force R
steps.content: By Newton's third law, the force R exerts on Q has the same magnitude, 5.0 N.

subtopic: first and second derivatives, simultaneous equations
question: A function f is defined by $f(x) = \dfrac{a}{x}+\dfrac{b}{x^2}$ where a and b are constants. It is given that $f'(1) = 2$ and $f''(-1) = -2$. What is the value of $a+b$?

steps.title: F x a x b x f a
Solving and subtracting gives then
steps.content: Solving $-a-2b=2$ and $-a+3b=-1$: subtracting gives $5b=-3 \Rightarrow b=-3/5$, then $-a = 2+2(-3/5) = 2-6/5=4/5 \Rightarrow a=-4/5$.

question:
An object is released from rest at a height $H$ above the ground and falls freely in a uniform gravitational field.

At time $t$ after being released, it has fallen a distance $s$ and is at a height $h$ above the ground, travelling at speed $v$.

The graph shows two quantities plotted.

| Row | y-axis | x-axis |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | $h$ | $t$ |
| 2 | kinetic energy | $h$ |
| 3 | gravitational potential energy | $s$ |

Which of the rows show(s) a possible pair of quantities for the axes?

(Assume that air resistance is negligible.)

steps.title: Read the given graph
steps.content: The plotted figure is a straight line of negative gradient with a positive $y$-intercept and a positive $x$-intercept.

steps.title: Reject height against time
steps.content: Row 1 plots $h$ against $t$, so $h=H-\tfrac{1}{2}gt^{2}$. That is a downward-opening parabola in $t$, not a straight line.

steps.title: Match kinetic energy to height
steps.content: Row 2: lost gravitational energy appears as kinetic energy, $E_{k}=mg(H-h)$. This decreases linearly in $h$, from $mgH$ at $h=0$ to $0$ at $h=H$.

steps.title: Match potential to distance fallen
steps.content: Row 3: $E_{p}=mg(H-s)$ measured from the ground is the same linear decreasing form in $s$. Rows 2 and 3 both match the figure.

solution.fast: Rows 2 and 3 are the same energy trade read from opposite ends of $H$, so they stand or fall together; only those two are linear.
solution.trap: Choosing the $h$ against $t$ pairing treats a quadratic free-fall graph as if it were a straight line.

question: Find the real value of x that satisfies $\log_3(x^2+3x+2) = 2+\log_3(x^2+2x)$.

steps.title: Checking domain arguments of logs must be positive
steps.content: Checking domain (arguments of logs must be positive): $x=-2$ makes $x^2+2x=0$ (invalid), so $x=1/8$.

subtopic: resistivity, resistance after re-forming a conductor
question: A 20 V battery of negligible internal resistance is connected in series with a 20 $\Omega$ resistor and a cylindrical conductor. The current in the circuit is 0.20 A. The cylindrical conductor is now removed, melted and re-formed into a new cylinder. After the cylinder has cooled to its original temperature, its length is 4 times greater than that of the original cylinder. What is the resistance of the new cylinder?

steps.title: When re formed with the same volume but
steps.content: When re-formed with the same volume but 4x the length, the cross-sectional area becomes $A/4$.

question: What is the constant term in the simplified binomial expansion of $\left(\dfrac{x}{4}+\dfrac{2}{x}\right)^8$?

subtopic: interference via reflection with phase change
question: A sound wave can travel from a source at P to a detector at R directly or by reflecting at Q. The angle between PR and PQ is 30° and the distance from P to R is d. [DIAGRAM: source P, detector R, reflecting surface with reflection point Q; angle QPR = 30°, distance PR = d]. There is a phase difference of $\pi$ radians between the incident and reflected wave at Q. Waves that reach R via Q are in phase with waves that reach R directly from P. Which expression gives the greatest wavelength of sound waves for which this is true?

steps.title: Find the geometric path difference
steps.content: Triangle $PQR$ is isosceles with $\angle QPR = \angle QRP = 30^\circ$, so $$PQ = QR = \dfrac{d/2}{\cos 30^\circ} = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{3}}.$$ The path difference is $$\delta = PQ + QR - PR = \dfrac{2d}{\sqrt{3}} - d = d\left(\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} - 1\right).$$

steps.title: Include the reflection phase shift
steps.content: A phase change of $\pi$ at $Q$ is equivalent to an extra path of $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$. For the two arrivals at the detector to be in phase, $$\delta + \dfrac{\lambda}{2} = n\lambda \quad (n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots).$$

steps.title: Maximise the wavelength
steps.content: Rearranging, $\lambda = \dfrac{\delta}{n - 1/2}$. The greatest wavelength is at $n = 1$: $$\lambda = 2\delta = 2d\left(\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} - 1\right).$$

subtopic: maximum value using auxiliary angle / Cauchy-Schwarz on a circle
question: Given that $x^2+y^2=1$, what is the greatest possible value of $2x+3y$?

steps.title: By the Cauchy Schwarz inequality or writing the
steps.content: By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (or writing $x=\cos\theta,y=\sin\theta$), the maximum of $ax+by$ subject to $x^2+y^2=1$ is $\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$.

question: Two springs have spring constants of 200 N/m and 600 N/m, respectively. The springs are joined in series, end-to-end, and stretched so that their combined extension is 0.80 m. What is the total strain energy stored in the springs?

steps.title: For springs in series the same force F
steps.content: For springs in series, the same force F acts through both, and total extension $x_{total} = F/k_1+F/k_2 = F\left(\dfrac{1}{200}+\dfrac{1}{600}\right)=F\times\dfrac{4}{600}=\dfrac{F}{150}$.

steps.title: Total strain energy J
steps.content: Total strain energy $=\tfrac12 F x_{total} = \tfrac12\times120\times0.80=48$ J.
